notes: Chef review wave 9. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: The one-pot Rwandan stew was broadly right. This pass uses the more common Igisafuliya spelling, keeps plantain, meat, and vegetables, and removes halal as a sourcing guarantee.
recommended_changes: Use plantains or green bananas with meat and vegetables.
Keep the one-pot simmer so plantain thickens the sauce.
Use Igisafuliya as the display name.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Rwandan references describe Igisafuliya as a one-pot stew with chicken or meat, plantains, potatoes, vegetables, and tomato.

sources.title: Main Street Farmers Market - Igisafuliya Rwandan Chicken Stew
UN Tourism - A Tour of African Gastronomy Rwanda

recipe.name: Igisafuliya
recipe.description: Rwandan igisafuliya, a one-pot stew of chicken or beef, green plantains, potatoes, tomatoes, carrots, and green beans.
recipe.story: Igisafuliya means the pot is doing the work. Let the plantain soften into the broth so the stew thickens naturally.
recipe.best_served_with: Rice, ubugali, avocado, fried plantains, or a simple tomato salad

recipes.review_summary: Isombe was missing the peanut butter that most Rwandan references expect. This pass adds peanut butter, green onion or pepper, and removes nut-free and halal tags.
recommended_changes: Use pounded cassava leaves as the base.
Add peanut butter near the end for body.
Keep eggplant, spinach, onion, garlic, and palm oil.
recipes.official_source_status: Rwandan nonprofit and food references consistently describe isombe as cassava leaves cooked with peanut butter and vegetables such as eggplant, spinach, onion, and bell pepper.

sources.title: Gondwana Ecotours - How to Make Isombe

recipe.description: Rwandan isombe, pounded cassava leaves simmered with peanut butter, palm oil, eggplant, spinach, onion, garlic, and pepper.
recipe.story: The peanut butter is what turns the greens into isombe. Add it after the leaves soften so it melts into a rich, smooth sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Ubugali, rice, boiled plantains, beans, or avocado

replace_steps.title: Start leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer pounded cassava leaves with water or vegetable stock until they begin to soften.

replace_steps.title: Cook vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: In palm oil, cook onion, garlic, eggplant, and bell pepper until soft and fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Combine
replace_steps.instruction: Stir the vegetable base into the cassava leaves. Add the whole chile if using.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in peanut butter until it melts into the sauce. Simmer until the leaves are tender and the stew is thick.

replace_steps.title: Add spinach
replace_steps.instruction: Fold in spinach or amaranth leaves and cook until wilted. Adjust salt and pepper before serving.

recipes.review_summary: Rwandan brochettes were sound but the marinade was a little generic. This pass centers goat, onion-on-skewer, tomato-onion-chile marinade, charcoal grilling, and removes halal guarantee.
recommended_changes: Use goat as the lead meat, with beef or chicken as alternatives.
Marinate in tomato, onion, garlic, chile, oil, and salt.
Serve with fried potatoes, plantains, or kachumbari-style tomato onion.
recipes.official_source_status: Rwandan recipe references consistently describe goat or beef brochettes grilled over charcoal and served with onion, tomato sauce, plantain, potatoes, or ugali.

recipe.description: Rwandan goat brochettes grilled over charcoal with tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and oil marinade.
recipe.story: Brochettes are bar food and celebration food at once. Keep the pieces even, grill over real heat, and serve them straight from the fire.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried potatoes, fried plantains, kachumbari, ugali, or cold beer

replace_steps.title: Marinate meat
replace_steps.instruction: Mix tomatoes, grated onion, garlic, chile if using, oil, salt, and pepper. Coat the meat and rest.

replace_steps.title: Thread skewers
replace_steps.instruction: Thread meat onto skewers with a few onion chunks, leaving small gaps for even heat.

replace_steps.title: Grill
replace_steps.instruction: Grill over hot charcoal, turning often, until smoky and cooked through.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve hot with fried potatoes or plantains and tomato-onion salad.

recipes.review_summary: Angú de banana is a simple, plausible São Toméan accompaniment to calulu. This pass keeps green bananas and palm oil but clarifies that breadfruit can stand in.
recommended_changes: Use green bananas or breadfruit.
Boil until very soft, then pound or mash smooth.
Finish with palm oil and salt.
recipes.official_source_status: Direct recipe sourcing is limited, but São Tomé food guides consistently pair calulu with mashed banana, breadfruit, or similar starchy sides.

sources.title: São Tomé and Príncipe Food Culture
Cuisine of São Tomé and Príncipe

recipe.description: São Toméan angú de banana, green bananas or breadfruit boiled soft, mashed smooth, and finished with palm oil and salt.
recipe.story: Angú should be smooth enough to carry calulu sauce. Pound while hot and loosen only with a little cooking water if needed.
recipe.best_served_with: Calulu, grilled fish, fish stew, or palm-oil vegetables

recipes.review_summary: Calulu was good but missing garlic, greens, chile, and cooking liquid in the ingredient list. This pass adds them and keeps the layered slow stew.
recommended_changes: Use dried or smoked fish with okra, tomato, eggplant, spinach or greens, garlic, onion, chile, and palm oil.
Soak salty fish before cooking.
Serve with angú, breadfruit, or rice.
recipes.official_source_status: São Tomé references consistently describe calulu as a national or emblematic dish of dried or smoked fish, okra, vegetables, palm oil, onion, garlic, and chile.

sources.title: Kreol Magazine - Calulu Traditional São Toméan Delight
Remitly - Calulu São Tomé and Príncipe National Dish
Cuisine of São Tomé and Príncipe

recipe.description: São Toméan calulu, dried or smoked fish slowly stewed with okra, tomatoes, eggplant, greens, garlic, chile, and palm oil.
recipe.story: Calulu is a layered pot. Let the fish, okra, greens, and palm oil settle into each other before stirring from the bottom.
recipe.best_served_with: Angú de banana, mashed breadfruit, rice, or boiled plantain

replace_ingredients.name: water or fish soaking liquid
replace_ingredients.prep_note: use only if not too salty

recipes.review_summary: The hot chocolate is a cacao showcase rather than a deeply standardized national recipe. This pass keeps São Tomé cacao central and allows water or milk so the drink can be rich without becoming too generic.
recommended_changes: Use São Tomé dark chocolate, cacao mass, or cocoa powder.
Heat gently with milk or water, sugar, cinnamon, vanilla, and a pinch of salt.
Whisk for body.
recipes.official_source_status: No official São Toméan hot chocolate standard found. São Tomé is strongly documented as a cacao origin, and high-cacao hot chocolate references support cacao, water or milk, sugar, and spice.

sources.title: São Tomé Expert - Chocolate and Cacao Plantations

recipe.description: São Tomé cacao hot chocolate made with dark chocolate or cacao mass, milk or water, cinnamon, vanilla, sugar, and a pinch of salt.
recipe.story: Let the cacao lead. Use enough liquid to make it drinkable, but simmer and whisk so it stays thick and glossy.
recipe.best_served_with: Banana fritters, coconut cake, roasted nuts, or fresh fruit

replace_ingredients.name: São Tomé dark chocolate, cacao mass, or cocoa powder
whole milk, oat milk, or water

replace_steps.title: Heat liquid
replace_steps.instruction: Warm milk or water with cinnamon until steaming but not boiling.

replace_steps.title: Melt cacao
replace_steps.instruction: Add chocolate or cacao and whisk until completely smooth.

replace_steps.title: Sweeten
replace_steps.instruction: Add sugar, vanilla if using, and a pinch of salt. Simmer gently until slightly thickened.

replace_steps.title: Whisk and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Remove cinnamon, whisk until glossy and foamy, and serve hot.

recipes.review_summary: Chicken yassa was ingredient-correct but the total time ignored its lemon-onion marinade. This pass increases prep time, adds bay leaf, and removes halal guarantee.
recommended_changes: Marinate chicken with lemon, onion, mustard, garlic, chile, and oil.
Grill or brown chicken before simmering.
Caramelize onions into a tangy sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: Senegalese sources consistently describe yassa as chicken or fish marinated in lemon, onion, mustard, garlic, and chile, then grilled or browned and simmered with onions.

recipe.description: Senegalese chicken yassa, grilled or browned chicken braised in caramelized onions, lemon, mustard, garlic, chile, and bay leaf.
recipe.story: The onions are the sauce. Give the lemon-mustard marinade time, then cook the onions down until sweet enough to balance the citrus.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, fried plantain, cucumber salad, or extra chile

recipes.review_summary: Mafe needed missing oil, garlic, ginger, water or stock, and optional okra or cabbage. This pass keeps peanut paste and tomato as the heart and removes halal guarantee.
recommended_changes: Brown meat, fry onion and tomato paste, then dissolve peanut butter into warm stock.
Use sweet potato, carrot, cabbage, okra, or potato as vegetables.
Simmer until the sauce is thick and glossy.
recipes.official_source_status: Senegalese and West African sources consistently describe mafé as a peanut paste stew with meat or chicken, tomato, root vegetables, onion, and stock or water.

recipe.description: Senegalese mafé, a peanut and tomato stew with beef, sweet potatoes, carrots, onion, garlic, ginger, and slow-cooked sauce.
recipe.story: Mafé should taste roasted and creamy, not like raw peanut butter. Fry the tomato paste, loosen the peanut paste with warm stock, then simmer patiently.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, couscous, or steamed millet

replace_ingredients.name: natural peanut butter or groundnut paste

recipes.review_summary: Thieboudienne was strong but missing onion, oil, carrot, okra, tamarind, and dried fish in the ingredient list even though the method mentioned them. This pass fills out the Senegalese roster.
recommended_changes: Stuff fish with rof or nokoss.
Use broken rice, tomato paste, vegetables, tamarind, dried fish, and fish stock.
Cook rice in the strained vegetable-fish broth.
recipes.official_source_status: Senegalese sources consistently identify thieboudienne as rice and fish with tomato broth, rof or nokoss, vegetables, tamarind, and sometimes dried or fermented fish.

sources.title: CIS Senegal - Thieboudienne Chef Samba Thiam

recipe.description: Senegalese thieboudienne, fish stuffed with rof and served over tomato rice cooked with cabbage, cassava, carrot, eggplant, okra, tamarind, and dried fish.
recipe.story: The rice is the prize. Once the fish and vegetables have flavored the broth, cook the rice in that same pot so every grain carries the sea and tomato.
recipe.best_served_with: Lime wedges, bissap, chile paste, or extra vegetables from the pot

replace_ingredients.name: whole grouper, sea bass, or firm fish

recipes.review_summary: The Seychellois grilled fish was sensible. This pass removes halal guarantee, uses whole fish or fillets, and adds lime and curry leaves as optional Creole notes.
recommended_changes: Use fresh reef fish, snapper, jobfish, or grouper.
Marinate with garlic, ginger, lemon or lime, and salt.
Serve with chunky tomato Creole sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Seychelles food references consistently describe grilled fresh fish with garlic, ginger, chile, herbs, lime, and Creole tomato sauce.

recipe.description: Seychellois grilled fish with garlic, ginger, lime, chile, and a chunky Creole tomato sauce.
recipe.story: Use the freshest fish you can find and do not bury it. The Creole sauce should brighten the fish, not hide it.
recipe.best_served_with: Rice, lentils, fried plantain, breadfruit, or cucumber salad

replace_ingredients.name: snapper, grouper, or firm fish fillets

recipes.review_summary: Ladob was accurate for the dessert version. This pass removes halal as irrelevant, keeps coconut milk, vanilla, nutmeg, plantain, and sweet potato, and mentions the savory form only in context.
recommended_changes: Use ripe plantain and sweet potato for the sweet version.
Simmer gently in coconut milk, sugar, vanilla, nutmeg, and salt.
Do not overcook into mush unless intentionally making a softer pudding.
recipes.official_source_status: Seychelles references consistently describe ladob as either sweet or savory. The dessert version uses ripe plantains or sweet potatoes simmered in coconut milk, sugar, vanilla, and spice.

recipe.description: Seychellois sweet ladob, ripe plantains and sweet potatoes simmered in coconut milk with vanilla, nutmeg, sugar, and salt.
recipe.story: Ladob should be creamy but still shaped. Simmer gently so the coconut milk thickens while the plantain and sweet potato stay intact.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, grilled fish meal finish, coconut flakes, or fresh fruit

recipes.review_summary: Shark chutney was mostly right. This pass removes halal guarantee and adds a practical firm-fish substitution while keeping shark, bilimbi, lime, onion, turmeric, ginger, and chile.
recommended_changes: Boil and flake shark or a firm fish substitute.
Cook with bilimbi or lime, onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, and chile.
Serve as a condiment or side with rice, lentils, or grilled fish.
recipes.official_source_status: Seychelles references consistently describe shark chutney or satini reken as boiled, skinned, flaked shark cooked with bilimbi, lime, onion, chile, and turmeric.

sources.title: The Creole Melting Pot - Shark Chutney Satini Reken

recipe.description: Seychellois shark chutney, or satini reken, made with boiled flaked shark, bilimbi, lime, onion, ginger, garlic, turmeric, and chile.
recipe.story: The chutney should be tangy and textured, not watery. Flake the fish fine, then cook it just enough for the bilimbi and spice to grip.
recipe.best_served_with: Rice, lentils, grilled fish, breadfruit, or Creole curry

replace_ingredients.name: shark fillet or firm white fish
